title: Pilau rice
subtitle: Yellow, cardamom-scented basmati rice
excerpt: Basmati rice cooked with whole cardamom pods, cinnamon, cloves, and turmeric until each grain is separate and fragrant. The curry-house standard accompaniment, distinct from plain boiled rice by its yellow colour and the whole spice fragrance absorbed during cooking. Made by the absorption method.
sourceNotes: Pilau rice (also pilaf, pulao) is a cooking technique for rice originating in the Middle East and South Asia. The British curry-house version is a simplified yellow rice cooked with whole spices, documented as a standard curry-house accompaniment throughout British-Indian restaurant literature from the 1970s onward.

content.text: Rinsing the rice is not optional. Rinse basmati under cold water until the water runs completely clear, this removes excess starch that causes the grains to clump together during cooking. The rice should then soak in cold water for 20 to 30 minutes before cooking, which allows even absorption and prevents the outer layer over-softening before the centre cooks through.

The absorption method is the most reliable for pilau rice at home: a specific ratio of water to rice, covered with a tight lid, and left to cook without lifting. The ratio for basmati is 1 part rice to 1.5 parts water. Once the water has been absorbed and the pan is steaming, turn off the heat and leave it undisturbed for 10 minutes, the residual heat finishes the cooking.
Fry the spices and cook the rice

Drain the soaked {{basmati-rice}}. Heat the {{ghee}} in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{cardamom-green}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, and {{cloves}} and fry for 30 seconds. Add the drained rice and stir to coat in the ghee and spices for 1 minute. Add 450 ml water, {{turmeric}}, and salt. Bring to the boil, stir once, then reduce the heat to the lowest setting. Cover with a tight-fitting lid and cook for 12 minutes without lifting the lid.

Turn off the heat and leave the pan, still covered, for 10 minutes. Do not lift the lid during this time. After resting, fluff the rice with a fork. Remove the whole spices before serving, or leave them in for the guest to set aside while eating.

items.fix: Rinse until the water runs completely clear. Do not lift the lid until the full rest time is complete
items.cause: Not rinsed enough or lid lifted during cooking

items.fix: Use exactly 450 ml water per 300 g of soaked, drained rice. If the base browns before the water absorbs, the heat is too high
items.cause: Insufficient water or heat too high burning the base before absorption
items.symptom: Rice is crunchy in the centre

items.fix: Use the 1:1.5 ratio. Soak for 20 to 30 minutes, not longer. Reduce water by 1 tablespoon if mushy
items.cause: Too much water or soaked too long

items.fix: Fry the whole spices in the ghee for 30 seconds before adding the rice, this blooms the aromatics. The turmeric gives colour; the cardamom gives fragrance
items.cause: Whole spices not fried first or turmeric omitted

content.text: Pilau rice is the curry-house table companion to every main dish. It sits in a different register from plain boiled basmati. The whole spices and ghee give it a fragrance that makes it worth eating alongside a curry, not just under one. The yellow colour comes from turmeric and signals the spiced cooking before you taste. In Pakistan and North India, pilau can be a dish in its own right, cooked with meat and stock. The British curry-house version is the simpler form.
